topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of damp earth and pine needles hit me first, a sudden, sharp jolt. I’d been walking along the edge of the woods, tracing the familiar line where my sapling used to stand. My breath hitched in my throat. Where the young oak, the one I’d painstakingly nurtured into a promising young tree as a child, should have been, there was only a stark, freshly cleared patch of mud. My hands, which had been gripping the rough bark of an old maple, began to tremble. I knelt down, pushing aside the fallen debris, my eyes scanning the ground with a frantic intensity. The silence of the clearing felt heavy, oppressive, as if the very air was holding its breath, waiting for something terrible to happen. A coldness seeped into my bones, and I couldn't seem to draw a full breath.

[story 2]

It was the sight that finally broke my composure. Standing where the sapling had been, now just a tangle of stumps and exposed roots, was an emptiness that felt profoundly wrong. I stood frozen, my gaze fixed on the barren earth. A slow, deep tremor started in my chest, radiating outwards until my fingers felt numb. "No," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy in the quiet afternoon. I took a hesitant step back, then another, my boots crunching on dry leaves, as if trying to physically distance myself from the scene. My jaw tightened, and I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks. Every instinct screamed at me to run, but my feet seemed rooted to the spot, trapped by a strange, paralyzing certainty that this was not how things were supposed to be.

[story 3]

I found the site by accident while clearing brush near the old creek bed. When I saw the bare patch of ground, my heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. I dropped the rake I was holding; the clatter was startlingly loud in the stillness. I stared at the remnants—the cut wood, the disturbed soil—and a wave of profound unease washed over me, leaving a clammy feeling on my skin. I walked slowly toward the devastation, my movements stiff and deliberate. I didn't look for a person; I just stood there, absorbing the finality of the removal, a hollow ache settling deep in my core. I kept rubbing my arms, a futile attempt to ward off the sudden chill that seemed to emanate from the empty space.
